data.title: Northwest Natural: Plenty Of Natural Growth Drivers To Justify A Higher Valuation
data.description:
Typically, energy-related stocks can be vulnerable to global market volatility and geopolitical disruptions. Yet, some thrive regardless of macroeconomic conditions because they are in household/business-staple niches with consistent demand. An example is Northwest Natural Holding Company (NYSE:NWN), the parent company of Northwest Natural Gas Company. It takes advantage of the important role it plays for residential, commercial, and industrial consumers. Its well-diversified business model and robust fundamentals are some attributes that drew me into it. Valuation is another aspect, as it trades cheaper than historical averages. Technicals conform as bullish momentum persists, but I suggest some caution about overbuying.

What Drives It Upside

As a new visitor to Northwest Natural Holding Company, I immediately became interested in its strengths that justify its current valuation and may support some price upside in the long run. These are the aspects I consider as a potential buyer of this stock.

1. Its Business Model Is Stable And Well-Diversified. NWN is a regulated utility company that delivers natural gas through its pipeline network. So, it generates a regulated return on its infrastructure investments, typically rate base. It doesn’t sell natural gas at a profit margin based on market prices or benchmarks. Although fluctuations in prices can affect it, NWN can easily cushion the unfavorable impact.

One is that NWN uses a pass-through pricing model. This means that it passes through natural gas costs to consumers. As a result, gas price volatility does not necessarily and directly squeeze margins. Second, NWN may also file rate adjustments with regulators to present wholesale gas cost fluctuations. This can be done annually or seasonally. Of course, this does not mean that NWN is completely insulated from the negative impact of natural gas prices. If the market price rises, consumers may reduce their usage, which may lower its delivery volume and revenues. Nonetheless, I believe that the effect is modest since natural gas is crucial in power consumption. This may be supported by weather since it can be a big factor in consumption changes. For instance, there may be more gas use during cold winters.

On the other hand, its water services are smaller and newer than the gas segment. Yet, it continues to expand. Running this business is similar to what it does in the gas segment since it owns and operates regulated water utilities through NW Natural Water. It’s also interesting to note how it operates its wastewater systems. It manages sewage collection/treatment and on-site/decentralized systems in certain places that are not connected to vast municipal sewers.

Considering this, I can say that NWN has quite an inelastic demand, which means that consumers heavily rely on it. Competition is there at some levels, but there are important caveats since it runs a regulated gas and water model. After all, its operations are located in defined territories under regulation like Oregon and Southwest Washington. And from its name, we can claim that NWN dominates these areas. This should not be surprising since it provides its service to about 2M people in 140 communities through 800 meters in these areas.

In addition, its recent performance speaks for all the things I explained. As you can see, its Q2 2025 revenues reached $236.2M, up by 11.5% YoY from $211.7M. Costs and expenses are very manageable through its pass-through model since they only increased by 6.5%. In addition, its operating leverage decreased from 37.2% to 30.9%, which means lower cost sensitivity. Indeed, it benefited from the lower natural gas price on a YoY basis since it lowered gas costs and enticed consumption. As a result, its operating margin rose from 7.4% to 11.5% YoY.

2. Expanding Business Means Higher Domestic Market Presence. It may have to compete with others for acquisitions of smaller utilities and even for new service territories. This is to ensure it can meet the increasing demand in the area it covers. This also helps if it wants to expand its coverage. For instance, it acquired SiEnergy Gas Utility to serve customers in the greater metropolitan areas in Texas. Considering its performance, I believe that NWN may sustain its operations there.

In my opinion, this is a strategic move because Texas had the highest natural gas consumption in 2023. This should not be surprising considering its population.

I also think it will have newer growth opportunities for NWN. Aside from its large population, Texas is known for its robust economy. With that, NWN may enjoy higher gas and water consumption in its commercial and industrial segments. Note that Texas has become more attractive for business relocations. From 2018 to 2023, Texas had a net change of 10 Fortune 500 Companies. It is also worth noting that seven of the eight companies that left California relocated to Texas. It is also among the favorites for startups.

3. Innovation And Transformation Can Ensure Longevity. Despite its solid market positioning, NWN does not stay complacent. In fact, it continues to transform itself as it embraces the potential of renewables. This positions it well amid the increasing call for cleaner energy sources, which may increase its appeal to pro-environment groups and government agencies.

4. AI/Digital Revolution May Bring Some Positive Spillovers. As businesses and households adapt to newer technologies, internet access becomes more crucial today. In turn, global data centers are expanding to meet the quick demand surge. In fact, data center capacity is expected to increase by 15% per year but still be insufficient to cover demand. So, what does it mean for NWN? Data centers will raise electricity consumption. In the US, about 40% of electricity comes from natural gas.

This may not have an immediate effect on NWN, especially in its household segment. After all, it does not primarily provide gas for electricity. But if the demand continues to surge and outpace supply, NWN may be used as a backup source. Logically, I think its gas may be used more for industrial and commercial use as data centers expand. It may be used in cogeneration systems for electricity and heat, although I think it will be small-scale initially.

5. Robust Fundamentals To Sustain Its Expansion. Considering its strong performance, it will not be surprising to see its sustainability. Its operating cash flow remains robust enough to cover its capex, dividends, and share repurchases. With that, its cash has increased in the past 11 quarters.

NWN complements it with prudent debt management. Its maturity profile is also decent and long, since the majority of the total amount has a maturity of more than a year. To be more specific, first mortgage bonds have maturity dates ranging from 2025 to 20253. Meanwhile, the maturity dates of NW Holdings’ unsecured bonds range from 2028 through 2034. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 5.2x, which means that NWN only needs about five years to repay the whole amount at once, even if it doesn’t have to. The wide maturity range offers NWN a lot of flexibility to make repayments on time while sustaining dividends. Hence, NWN has enough capital buffer to sustain its business operations and dividends.

Possible Risks

In its business model, I believe that its risks are quite limited. Yet, stubborn inflation may still have an impact on its performance. It can affect gas and water consumption in many households and businesses. People may become more conscious about their spending and household expenses. And one way to save money is by conserving energy and water. Businesses may do the same, and I think this is more applicable to water consumption.

Another thing to consider is the potential competition and regulation it may face in Texas. Although it can already be considered an established company, NWN is still new to Texas. So, it may have to adjust and comply with the state policies. It may incur additional costs as it makes new pricing and marketing strategies to stabilize revenues and margins.

Valuation/Projection

NWN has already rebounded after its continued price decrease from 2020 to 2024. At $46.66, it now trades at 18x its EPS, which is lower than the five-year average of 20x. If we use this to find its fair value, the target price, or TP, will be $50.33. Even if we add a 5% risk consideration, the TP of $47.81 shows that NWN has a cheaper valuation than in previous years. The same goes for its book value using P/B, which gives a TP of $53.70. Lastly, its P/S using sales per share suggests a TP of $51.27. These price ratios suggest that NWN still has some upside potential. And I believe that’s justifiable considering the points I explained earlier.

Less than two weeks before its Q3 2025 release. While it's quite early to expect something from it, I believe that it was able to sustain its YoY growth in last quarter. One easy answer is the added revenue impact of SiEnergy. Another factor is that the price of natural gas increased YoY. So, it can mean higher pricing for NWN. Likewise, I think it's logical to expect higher costs and expenses since it has a larger operating capacity today on top of cost pressures brought upon by inflation. But since it still bases its pricing mainly on its costs, I believe NWN can stabilize its margins.

To have a more forward-looking view, I also estimated its potential performance. Based on its YoY performance in recent quarters. I believe that expecting 10% revenue growth for the whole year is sound. With that, I derived a revenue of $1.27B. This is more conservative than the analysts’ estimate of $1.35B. To compare the two possible outcomes, I used my estimated revenue as my low estimate, while the analyst’s estimate will be the high estimate. I also set COGS and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue at 36% and 48%, respectively. These are based on their recent trends. I also projected depreciation with the straight-line method to get the EBITDA. As you can see, NWN will remain profitable. And if we use the current price of NWN, P/S FWD low and high will be 1.50x ($46.66/$31.08) and 1.42x ($46.66/$32.93). Either way, they will still be lower than the historical P/S average. This proves that NWN still trades at a reasonable level.

Technicals

NWN’s technicals are still clearly bullish as the price keeps increasing with new highs and lows. It is also above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the uptrend still holds. Also, the 50-day SMA still increases, so market sentiments are still optimistic.

MACD and RSI are still consistent with the trend, so there is no bearish divergence yet. The MACD line is still increasing above the signal line. This means that buying volume is still increasing. It is also within positive territory along with the histogram, which shows that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. Meanwhile, RSI is back above 70 after buyers took advantage of the slight price dip two weeks ago. My concern is that the stock is already overbought, which may prompt profit-takers to act on it and lead to some dips.

Conclusion

Northwest Natural Holding Company is a very compelling stock with a strategic business model and solid growth drivers to justify its bullish trend. Its robust performance and fundamentals also support its increasing operating capacity and capex while sustaining dividends. In addition, it trades at a reasonable price with upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as the rally continues, but investors may wish to wait a bit as the stock becomes overbought. I’m giving this stock a solid buy rating.

data.title: Aristotle Small/Mid Cap Equity WM Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

U.S. small/mid cap equities delivered robust performance in the third quarter of 2025, with the Russell 2500 Index reaching its first all-time high since November 2024. The rally was driven by a generally favorable macroeconomic backdrop, including a 25 basis point rate cut by the Federal Reserve in September, which marked a shift toward more accommodative policy amid signs of labor market softening. U.S. GDP growth remained strong at 3.8% in Q2, supported by resilient consumer spending and the easing of trade tensions following tariff rollbacks. Inflation edged higher to 2.9%, driven in part by energy costs and tariff passthroughs, but remained within a manageable range for businesses and consumers. Sentiment was risk-on, fueled by Artificial Intelligence driven optimism as companies with AI exposure or disruptive potential saw disproportionate gains, as investors chased momentum and future earnings potential over traditional valuation metrics. Small/mid-caps benefited from dovish Fed rhetoric, attractive relative valuations, broadening of market breadth, earnings recovery and a rotation away from mega cap stocks.

Stylistically, growth stocks outperformed their value counterparts during the quarter as the Russell 2500 Growth Index returned 10.73% compared to the 8.17% return of the Russell 2500 Value index. This continues the 2024 trend where growth significantly outperformed value.

From a factor perspective, lower quality companies significantly outperformed higher quality companies during the quarter. Factors that had the strongest payoffs were high beta, momentum, higher volatility, non-earners, high sales growth, micro caps, cyclical, and highly shorted stocks.

The best performing sectors were Information Technology (+14.47%), Health Care (+12.37%), and Utilities (+12.30%), while the worst performing sectors were Consumer Staples (-3.77%), Financials (+4.02%), and Real Estate (+5.79%).

Sources: CAPS Composite Hub, Russell InvestmentsPast performance is not indicative of future results. Composite returns are presented pure gross and net of the maximum wrap fee and include the reinvestment of all income. Pure gross returns do not reflect the deduction of any trading costs or other fees and are supplemental to the net returns. Net returns are calculated by subtracting the highest applicable wrap/SMA fee, which includes trading costs and custodial fees, from the pure gross monthly composite return (2.00% on an annual basis, or 0.17% monthly). Composite returns are preliminary pending final account reconciliation. Please see important disclosures at the end of this document.

Performance Review

For the third quarter of 2025, the Aristotle Small/Mid Cap Equity Composite generated a total return of 2.57% net of fees (3.07% pure gross of fees), underperforming the 9.00% total return of the Russell 2500 Index. The largest detractor from relative performance was security selection in Health Care (a combination of stocks we owned coupled with not owning biotechnology), Industrials and Materials. This was partially offset by strong security selection in Consumer Discretionary coupled with an overweight allocation to Information Technology and an underweight allocation to Financials.

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Ciena (CIEN) Haemonetics (HAE)
Alamos Gold (AGI) MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI)
Supernus Pharmaceuticals (SUPN) Gartner (IT)
Wolverine World Wide (WWW) Baldwin Insurance (BWIN) Group
Advanced Energy Industries (AEIS) James Hardie Industries (OTCPK:JHIUF)

Contributors

Ciena (CIEN), is an optical networking equipment manufacturer for telecommunications and web scale network operators. The stock was bolstered due to strong earnings results and optimistic forward guidance driven by AI-related demand and technological innovation. We believe the company is well-positioned to benefit from strong demand for bandwidth given its differentiated product portfolio and history of technological innovation, which should lead to strong operating results and shareholder value creation.

Alamos Gold (AGI-CA), engages in the exploration, development, mining and extraction of precious metals. The company benefited from rising gold prices as investors purchased the commodity as an inflation hedge. We maintain our investment as we believe in the company’s lower geopolitical risk profile, solid production growth plan from 600k oz to 1M oz per year over the next five years, and strong operational track record.

Detractors

Haemonetics (HAE), is a global provider of hematology and blood management products and solutions. The company delivered mixed results during the quarter, beating analyst earnings estimates but posting weaker than expected revenue results. We believe that the company’s strong competitive position within the plasmapheresis market along with increased investment in research and development should create value for shareholders over a multi-year period.

MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI), is a designer and manufacturer of high-performance semiconductor products. Despite reporting strong Q3 earnings, the stock declined as investors reacted to a slight margin impact from a recent acquisition and a slower-than-expected recovery in the industrial market—both short-term deviations from analyst forecasts. We maintain our position, as we believe the company’s meaningful exposure to growing demand from Data Center and 5G end market applications along with the integration of recent acquisitions and domestic manufacturing footprint should drive additional shareholder value in periods to come.

Recent Portfolio Activity

Buys/Acquisitions Sells/Liquidations
Columbia Banking System (COLB) 1-800-FLOWERS.COM
Essential Utilities ALLETE
Hilton Grand Vacations AZEK
James Hardie Industries Carter’s (CRI)
Pinnacle West Capital (PNW) Pacific Premier Bancorp
Primo Brands (PRMB) NCR Voyix
Service Corporation International

Buys/acquisitions

Columbia Banking System (COLB), is a Washington state based commercial bank that is in the early stages of winding down a large portfolio of low-yielding loans with organically driven higher yielding relationship loans and lower cost core deposit funding. A combination of operational cost savings associated with the Pacific Premier Bancorp merger and an increased focus on increasing fee-based income should allow for higher than historical profit margins and an attractive EPS growth outlook, ultimately allowing for incremental share buybacks or dividend increases.

Essential Utilities (WTRG), is a Pennsylvania-based utility providing water, wastewater, and natural gas services across nine states through its Aqua and Peoples Gas subsidiaries. Rate base expansion should continue to be driven by ongoing infrastructure investments and municipal water system acquisitions. A favorable regulatory landscape in the company’s core markets, including Pennsylvania and Texas, supports operational stability and consistent dividend growth. The company is a designated “Dividend Aristocrat”, having raised its dividend for 34 straight years.

Hilton Grand Vacations (HGV) is a leading global timeshare company that develops, markets and operates a system of brand-name, high-quality vacation ownership resorts in select vacation destinations, under the brand names: Hilton Vacation Club, Hilton Grand Vacation Club, Hilton Club. HGV’s proprietary relationship with Hilton Worldwide (HLT) Holdings (HLT) allows it to market exclusively to HLT’s 180 mil Hilton Honors members to capitalize on growing demand for travel. Recent acquisitions broadened the company resort base to include properties appealing to higher income consumers.

James Hardie Industries (JHX), manufactures Fiber Cement and Fiber Gypsum products (siding, backerboard, etc.) used primarily in residential construction (newbuild and repair/remodel). JHX acquired the existing portfolio company, The AZEK Company (AZEK), which provides JHX with a broader product offering for distributors, contractors and homeowners, positioning the company for market share gains in an expanded addressable market. Operating synergies, merger cost synergies and enhanced scale efficiencies should further enhance the shareholder value creation potential.

Pinnacle West Capital (PNW), is an Arizona-based, vertically integrated electric utility company serving central Arizona. Population migration and the expansion of industrial manufacturing within the Phoenix metropolitan area is driving increased energy demand and capital investment opportunities for the company. An improving regulatory backdrop in Arizona should also allow for reduced earnings volatility and accelerated cost recovery moving forward.

Primo Brands (PRMB), is a branded beverage company focused on healthy hydration. The company is the top player in U.S. bottled water with approximately 19% market share. The portfolio of brands is well diversified across distribution channels, product format, sizes and price points that allow the company to cost efficiently reach 90% of the U.S. population. Increasing consumption of bottled water should be supported by concerns about municipal tap water quality, the wellness movement leading to lower consumption of soda, juice, and alcoholic beverages, and innovation in water flavors and premium products attracting consumers toward the category.

Service Corporation International (SCI), is North America’s largest provider of deathcare products and services. The company is strategically positioned to benefit from long-term demographic tailwinds driven by the aging Baby Boomer population. Given the high fixed-cost nature of the business, rising volumes are expected to deliver operating leverage, driving margin expansion and increased cash generation. Total shareholder value is further enhanced by a balanced capital allocation strategy, featuring dividends, share repurchases, and accretive acquisitions that strengthen the company’s market leadership.

Sells/liquidations

1-800-FLOWERS.COM (FLWS), is an e-commerce provider of floral and gift products. The position was liquidated due to an increasingly uncertain fundamental outlook.

ALLETE (ALE), is a Minnesota based electric utility company. The position was liquidated ahead of its pending acquisition by a private equity consortium.

AZEK (AZEK), is a manufacturer of composite decking products for primarily residential applications. The company was acquired by James Hardie Industries plc (JHX).

Carter’s (CRI), is a manufacturer, distributor and retailer of infant and young children’s apparel. The position was liquidated due to deteriorated fundamental performance and an uncertain strategic outlook.

Pacific Premier Bancorp (PPBI), a California based bank holding company, was acquired by Columbia Banking System (COLB).

NCR Voyix (VYX), is a provider of digital commerce solutions to the retail, restaurants, and digital banking industries. The position was liquidated due to deteriorating fundamental performance following the spin out from NCR Corporation (VYX).

Outlook

We remain optimistic about the long-term potential for the small/mid-cap segment of the U.S. market. Valuations remain compelling relative to large caps, with the Russell 2500 Index trading near the lower end of its historical range. Potential tailwinds, including deregulation, lower corporate tax rates, increased M&A activity, continued reshoring of U.S. manufacturing, and infrastructure-related spending, could provide additional support for small/mid-cap stocks. Volatility remains elevated over concerns around inflationary risks, geopolitical tensions, and potential U.S. economic and labor weakness.

Positioning

Our current positioning is a function of our bottom-up security selection process and our ability to identify what we view as attractive investment candidates, regardless of economic sector definitions. Overweights in Information Technology and Materials are mostly a function of our underlying company specific views rather than any top-down predictions for each sector. Conversely, we continue to be underweight in Consumer Discretionary, as we have been unable to identify what we consider to be compelling long-term opportunities that fit our discipline given the rising risk profiles of many retail businesses and a potential deceleration in goods spending following a period of strength. We are also underweight in Financials as the sector has experienced strong returns, leading us to harvest gains and redeploy the proceeds to what we consider to be more attractive reward to risk opportunities. Given our focus on long-term business fundamentals, our patient investment approach and low portfolio turnover, the strategy’s sector positioning generally does not change significantly from quarter to quarter. However, we may take advantage of periods of volatility by adding selectively to certain companies when appropriate.

data.title: AI Killed The Internet Star But Wiley Offers A Pure Data Hoard
data.description:
It's estimated that up to 57% of text content on the Internet was written and/or translated by Artificial Intelligence. Some believe that this number will rise to 90% by 2026. Interestingly enough, researchers at Oxford found that when generative AI relies solely on content crafted by AI programs, the response quality degrades. In fact, by the ninth consecutive query, the AI model collapsed. With time, AI model developers will probably improve this, but I suspect that AI models will either get immensely repetitive or nonsensical, or both, if their "diet" is only AI-generated content.

Which brings us to John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (simply Wiley henceforth). Wiley (NYSE:WLY) is one of the largest education publishers in the world. Crucially, Wiley owns a huge number of academic journals and has published countless hefty textbooks. In science, "low background" steel is crucial for many applications. This steel wasn't exposed to nuclear bomb testing, which essentially infected steel the world over with radiation. Wiley's already-generated content, and especially content written before generative AI went public, can be viewed as something similar: high-quality, peer-reviewed content written by experts that has not been infected with AI.

I believe that pure data "hoards" will be increasingly important as time passes, which should create licensing opportunities for companies like Wiley. Further, as we move into more specific and advanced applications, such as diagnosing diseases or providing medical advice, niche expertise, which Wiley can provide via its own authors, will also become all the more important. With that in mind, I rate Wiley a cautious buy. I do believe the company has good long-term prospects, but in the near term, it may slide a bit further.

Will Artificial Intelligence Kill The Internet?

Video killed the radio star, and now AI may destroy the World Wide Web. Dead Internet Theory postulates that starting in 2016, the presence of bots and AI began to grow rapidly, and by 2021, some believe that such non-organic content was actually the majority of content online. Meanwhile, in the Cyberpunk Universe, the first iteration of the Internet actually had to be abandoned because it was taken over by AI. Dead Internet Theory sounds a bit conspiratorial, while Cyberpunk is fiction, but the vast quantities of AI-generated content flooding into the World Wide Web may degrade the overall quality of the Internet.

AI models scrape the web for vast amounts of content to feed into their systems. Since it can be difficult, if not impossible, to distinguish between AI-generated content and human-written content, it's quite likely that many AI models are now being trained on content that was itself generated by AI. Especially in cases where AI gets something wrong, plugging in the wrong numbers for economic metrics, mixing up dates, and otherwise hallucinating, the risk of errors being magnified could be very high.

Better data will likely produce higher-quality AI models and generated content. Yet even major publications are at risk of becoming infected with AI, and it's quite possible that content coming out in academic journals (whether intentionally or unintentionally) these days has been infected as well. Content from before the AI era thus offers one of the safer ways to get pure human-written content.

Wiley is now leaning into AI, offering licensing agreements that allow AI developers to port Wiley's content into their models. AI developers have faced legal actions over using copyrighted materials to train models. By licensing through Wiley, AI developers can avoid this pitfall. Wiley is targeting Foundational Large Language Model (LLM) Training, Fine-Tuning Customized Models, and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). Wiley also aims to pay authors, which should help ease tensions over content use.

Niche AI Applications Require Niche Data

Harvard Medical School recently announced a deal with Microsoft to use consumer health content for AI applications. Right now, everyone is investing in AI, yet most applications are still pretty primitive. Microsoft's Copilot program will use content to then answer health-related questions for users. Ultimately, the quality of answers provided by AI will largely depend on the quality of content used to train and sustain AI models.

I suspect we'll see more university agreements going forward. I also believe we'll see more partnerships with companies like Wiley. Importantly, AI licensing will allow Wiley to get more out of its content without raising costs by much. The content is already generated, and Wiley has already built a profitable business around textbooks and journals. Figuring out new ways to monetize content could quickly improve margins and add to the bottom line.

WLY Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

Wiley has been on a slide, and currently it is suffering from declining revenues (-8.50%). College enrollment has trended downward in recent years, with more students questioning the value of college degrees. Inflation and tight consumer budgets also make expensive textbooks all the more unaffordable. Without the potential for AI licensing, I would probably rate WLY a sell or, at best, a hold. WLY has been beating earnings estimates regularly but did fall short in Q1 2026.

Wiley's GAAP PE ratio is a bit high at 21.13, but the non-GAAP ratio is a more appetizing 10.25 (Seeking Alpha data). Price/Sales (TTM) is also quite healthy at 1.21. The Gross Profit Margin (TTM) is an excellent 74.18%, but the net income margin (TTM) comes back down to earth at 5.82%. As mentioned earlier, I believe that Wiley may be able to bolster its margins through AI licensing.

Wiley also offers a solid dividend with a 3.85% yield. I'm currently more focused on growth rather than income, but dividends here do improve the buy case given my concerns. The 5-year dividend growth weighs in at 0.72%, although with revenues on the decline, one has to wonder if Wiley will cut or pause the dividend at some point.

Seeking Alpha's quant tools give Wiley a strong sell rating, owing in part to EPS revisions. I do believe this stock presents moderately high risks for now. I can get over the EPS revisions, but the declining revenue does make me nervous. Still, I think the AI catalyst here is intriguing enough to merit a risk capital investment if nothing else. If Wiley can slow or, better yet, reverse its negative revenue growth, my outlook would likely improve.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The AI bears might be proven right, and AI could amount to much ado about nothing. I find this unlikely, but we can't rule it out. I think Wiley may be closing in on its floor and that revenue growth will improve, but I may be overestimating the stock. Continued sustained revenue declines, or worse, accelerating revenue declines, could send WLY tumbling.

Key Takeaway: Wiley is Worth a Risk Capital Bet

Is the Internet dying? At the very least, I believe pure human data and content will be crucial in a World Wide Web infected with AI. Wiley has one of the richest pure data hoards, which I believe the company will effectively monetize. Since the cost of monetizing this content should be relatively low, this could lead to quickly improving margins. While the risks are moderately high here, the upside is strong as well.

data.title: Compelling Value In Small-Cap Equities And Select Cyclicals
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Ariel Focus Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Although lingering policy and geopolitical risks may contribute to heightened volatility, investor sentiment remains upbeat; and we continued to view macroeconomic developments through the lens of our long-term investment horizon. Against this backdrop, Ariel Focus Fund surged +20.76% higher in the quarter, trouncing the +5.33% gain posted by Russell 1000 Value Index and the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index's +8.12% return.

Top Contributors

Supplier of residential thermal, comfort and security solutions, Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) was the top contributor in the quarter driven by strong earnings and a subsequent raise in guidance. Organic revenue, EBITDA and adjusted EPS all exceeded expectations. The company also finalized its agreement with Honeywell (HON), eliminating future financial obligations under their indemnification arrangement. In our view, REZI remains undervalued, with long-term growth supported by rising demand for connected smart home solutions and continued product innovation.

Shares of gold mining company, Barrick Mining Corporation (B) also jumped in the quarter on strong financial results buoyed by rising gold prices. The company also announced the strategic divestiture of its Hemlo Gold Mine to Carcetti Capital for $1.09 billion, a move aimed at streamlining its asset portfolio and sharpening operational focus. Barrick's solid liquidity position continues to underpin its commitment to shareholder returns, reflected in ongoing dividends and share repurchase activity. In a notable leadership transition, President and CEO Mark Bristow will step down after nearly seven years of service. The company has initiated a formal search for his successor.

Additionally, manufacturer and distributor of medical devices specializing in dental products, ZimVie, Inc. (ZIMV), advanced following an announcement it will be acquired by ARCHIMED, a leading healthcare-focused investment firm, with the transaction expected to close by year-end.

Top Detractors

In contrast, producer of crop nutrients, Mosaic Co. (MOS) , was the biggest detractor from performance in the period following an earnings miss that came after a strong share price run. Higher potash and phosphate prices were offset by lower volumes and rising production costs. Despite near-term pressures, phosphate markets are expected to remain tight through 2025 due to limited new supply. MOS remains focused on cost discipline, free cash flow generation, and preserving its investment-grade credit profile, while continuing to return significant capital to shareholders.

New holding and distributor of over-the-counter pharmaceutical drugs and products, Prestige Consumer Healthcare Inc. (PBH), also declined following mixed earnings results and a downward revision to full year guidance. Sales were impacted by limited inventory in the eye care segment, but the announced acquisition of Pillar5, a leading sterile ophthalmic manufacturer, is expected to strengthen supply over time. While ophthalmic recovery will be gradual, PBH reaffirmed its free cash flow outlook, supporting continued share repurchases and strategic acquisitions. Outside of eye care, the core portfolio delivered as expected, including gross margin expansion. We believe PBH has room to close its valuation gap relative to peers in the consumer healthcare sector.

Lastly, Schlumberger Limited (SLB) traded lower during the quarter, pressured by a challenging macroeconomic environment. OPEC+ supply increases and heightened geopolitical uncertainty weighed on oil prices, dampening upstream investment activity. Revenue softness and margin pressure in the Reservoir Performance and Well Construction segments impacted results, despite solid contributions from Digital and Production Systems services. Looking ahead, we see medium-term tailwinds as national oil companies accelerate investment in long-cycle projects to offset expected production declines. With unmatched scale, broad technical capabilities, and strong exposure to resilient international markets, we believe SLB remains the best-positioned oilfield services provider to meet rising global energy demand.

New Positions

In addition to Prestige Consumer Healthcare (PBH) discussed above, we initiated two new positions in the quarter.

We added Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (AJG), the world's largest insurance broker focused on middle-market clients. Shares came under modest pressure during the quarter due to a delay in closing its $13.4 billion acquisition of AssuredPartners. The deal ultimately closed in August 2025, with management now projecting stronger synergies than initially anticipated. Looking ahead, we believe AJG's scale, specialized expertise, and highly scalable platform position it well for continued organic growth and margin expansion. The resilience of the global insurance market further supports its long-term outlook, reinforcing our confidence in AJG as a core holding.

We also purchased Fiserv, Inc. (FI), leading global provider of payment processing and financial services technology solutions. The company possesses unmatched scale and cross-selling abilities across its businesses, including its core financial technology solutions, merchant acceptance and payment processing. Additionally, these innovative technologies are deeply entrenched in client operations, providing attractive and predictable recurring economics representative of a wide moat, high switching cost service business. A recent pullback in the stock provided an attractive entry point. Shares came under pressure due to investor concerns around Clover volumes decelerating. However, the deceleration was due to one-time items and growth is expected to accelerate later this year. In our view, FI offers a rare opportunity to own a best-in-class financial technology business that should benefit from the secular demand for innovative financial technology.

Sells

By comparison, we sold our position in manufacturer and distributor of medical devices specializing in dental products, ZimVie, Inc. on valuation. We also exited oil services company, Core Laboratories, Inc. (CLB) and manufacturer and developer of laboratory equipment and biological testing, Bio-Rad Laboratories Inc. (BIO) to pursue more compelling opportunities.

data.title: Boston Scientific Q3: Operational Improvements Warrant An Upgrade To A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX) recently reported its Q3 numbers, so I wanted to quickly take a look at them in more detail, as well as give some comments on the outlook and why I think there is a bright future ahead for BSX, leading me to change my rating to a buy.

Last time I covered the company, I gave it a hold rating, saying that the growth wasn’t good enough to justify its high P/E ratio at that time. Since then, the company’s share matched the performance of the S&P 500 (SPY).

By the Numbers

Sales came in at $5.07B, which is up a decent 20% y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by $100m. Looking at the revenue breakdown in more detail, endoscopy revenues came in at $747m, up 10% y/y. Urology came in at $682, with a whopping 28.1% growth y/y, leading to MedSurg performance to 16.4% for the quarter. Cardiology performed very well too, coming in at 24% y/y growth to $2.6B, helping the Cardiovascular segment to grow at 22.4%. The cardiovascular segment’s performance can be attributed to the strong global demand and innovations like WATCHMAN technology and the Farapulse PFA system. The MedSurg segment’s performance was lifted by the company’s urology department, as the treatments of underserved patient populations with kidney stones, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and other male stress urinary incontinence were the main reasons, as well as past acquisitions leading to a much higher inorganic growth compared to organic (16.4% vs 7.6%).

Looking at the region breakdown in revenues, the US market showed particular strength, with reported revenue growing at around 27% y/y, while APAC sales grew a respectable 17% also with double-digit expansion in Japan and China. EMEA, on the other hand, saw a slight organic decline due to shifts in business and currency impact. Emerging markets came in at a respectable 11.5%, so overall, decent performance all around.

Let’s have a look at the company’s margin progress so far. Q3 EPS of $0.75 beat analyst estimates by 4 cents. Adjusted gross margins came in at 71%, up around 60 bps from the same time last year, supported by a favorable product mix. Operating margins came in at 20.7%, which increased by an impressive 330 bps compared to last year, despite the ongoing tariff-related headwinds, which were estimated to be $100m. The management did a commendable job of controlling costs and making cuts exactly where the company was bloated.

On top of these results, the management raised full-year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance to $3.02-$3.04, and organic revenue growth to around 15.5%. Q4 ’25 revenue is expected to be between $5.22B-$5.31B, while the adjusted EPS guidance is in the range of $0.77-$0.79. These growth expectations continue to lean on the momentum in the cardiovascular segment, especially thanks to products like WATCHMAN, Farapulse, and EP momentum.

Overall, the company’s impressive performance continued this past quarter, too. A beat on the top and bottom lines, as well as a raise in guidance, is exactly what the investors are looking for. It can’t get better than that. Naturally, investors sent the company’s shares up on the day by as much as 5%, only to have some retracement since, but investor reception has been positive.

Comments on the Outlook

It seems the company is in a really great place, growth-wise. The products have been gaining traction, and the management has been very active in making further innovations and upgrades to the already existing suite of products. Clearly, the WATCHMAN and Farapulse treatment systems are in rather high demand, helping people of many ages to live longer by addressing the leading causes of death in adults. Heart disease is responsible for 1 in 3 deaths annually. That is a horrific number. Strokes account for roughly another 17% of deaths in the US. So, further improvements in these spaces are essential. The company is not standing around until some other company comes up with a much better product to help fight these medical problems.

The current iteration of the WATCHMAN, called FLX, already features improvements like a shorter length, with less metal exposure, and more size options. The upcoming WATCHMAN Elite, which is due to be released in late 2027 or early 2028, is the next step in maintaining the company’s edge in the field, which should provide even more stability, adaptability, and a better fit for a wider range of heart anatomies. This new device could also expand the company’s TAM because there may be people who tolerate long-term blood thinners but still want to have an alternative stroke risk reduction option.

With the future iterations of these products, we can expect the company’s cardiovascular revenue segment to grow at an impressive 15%-20%+ for the next several years, and that is not counting all the other company’s revenue segments, which are showing a lot of growth already.

The company’s M&A strategy is working wonders since we saw there was a lot of inorganic growth. The next upcoming closing of a deal is Nalu Medical, which focuses on peripheral nerve stimulation for chronic pain management. This is going to complement BSX’s neuromodulation therapies, helping reach more people who have chronic pain. Nalu Medical’s PNS therapy tests of COMFORT showed that 87% of patients reported more than a 50% reduction in pain at 12 months, while COMFORT 2 showed that 79% of patients reached pain relief of 64% just after 6 months. This looks like a very promising therapy and should help the company’s top line in the future quite a bit, as the expectation of growth for NALU in 2026 stands at around 25%.

BSX’s M&A strategy is central to its growth outlook, and it seems that the management knows exactly what companies to acquire to continue to gain synergies and maintain its market position or even gain market share overall.

Overall, the company seems to be positioned for a lot of growth over the next year and to at least maintain this momentum until 2028. By then, I’m sure there will be another big product that will continue to help its operations further.

Valuation

Let’s look at a quick DCF model to see what a good price is to pay for the company right now. I’ll keep the assumptions quite simple and not too optimistic or conservative. Below are the assumptions.

For revenue growth, I can see the company could continue to reach such digits over the next 5 years rather easily. I think the growth is probably a bit on the lower end of possible outcomes, but that’s fine. For operating margin, I can see the company reaching 26% rather easily as well, since in just a year, BSX managed to improve it by 3.3%. This way, my EPS assumptions of around $3.00 match the company’s outlook for next year. The rest of the assumptions I kept according to the company’s historical outcomes. With these rather conservative estimates, I am ok with using the company’s low WACC of 6.8% as my discount rate. I am not requiring much here.

With that said, the company seems to be trading below its fair value and has a decent upside.

Risks to the thesis

Tariffs may still be relevant, even though right now it seems that the management has done a great job navigating it all so far. You never know what is going to happen once Trump decides to do something about medical products coming in from countries like China, Mexico, and Canada. Many components of the BSX products are made in those countries, so at some point, if things go further south, expect more headwinds and a hit to margins. So far, the strong sales growth has more than offset the current tariff situation.

More of a long-term risk is that the future products could be delayed due to regulatory and approval decisions. The company relies on new product launches to continue growing, so any delays or denials will weigh on the company’s growth outlook, as well as its share appreciation potential.

Additionally, the M&A strategy has been working well, but there may be some integration risks in the future, in turn could lead to no synergies and more unnecessary costs due to failed integrations.

Negative trial results for its upcoming products could massively affect the company’s ability to grow, which will lead to a loss in confidence by investors and a lower share price.

Closing Comments

Overall, I believe the company will continue to navigate the macroeconomic situation well, and the future products seem very promising, which will help the company lead the way in cardiovascular disease. I don’t see the company going away any time soon, and being in such a demanding medical field, the company’s products remain essential for saving lives. I am upgrading Boston Scientific to a buy.

data.title: CorMedix: Strong Buy After Strong Developments And A Strategy Before Earnings
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) at the end of June and assigned it a Buy status. CorMedix is a small biotech company that produces DefenCath, an antimicrobial catheter lock solution for reducing the incidence of catheter-related bloodstream infections [CRBSI] in adult patients receiving chronic hemodialysis through a central venous catheter.

With the stock at ~$12.5 and a base-case Fair Value estimate of $26.2, I initiated a small position. I kept my position limited to 0.3x due to their reliance on a single product. Indeed, the potential reward is higher (perhaps much higher in time) than the potential risk. That’s why I like to have many such small biotech names in my portfolio. Even if one of them fails, on average it’s very likely to have high growth from products in incipient phases.

There were two important developments in the meantime:

  • CorMedix completed the acquisition of Melinta Therapeutics, a private commercial-stage company providing innovative therapies for acute and life-threatening illnesses.
  • FY 2025 pro forma net revenue guidance was raised from the previously announced range of $325M–$350M to at least $375M.

I am raising both my Fair Value estimate and my position slightly. I also think that it’s a good moment to buy a Call option for about 25% of my position, with the intention to sell it before the next earnings, when IV (Implied Volatility) is expected to be higher.

Business Highlights

This is what the CorMedix portfolio looks like after the Melinta acquisition:

In my initial article I analysed in detail the market and competition for DefenCath, so I will summarize:

  • Starting from ~500K hemodialysis patients in the US, with 3 treatments per week, 2 vials per treatment, and taking about 25% as potential market, we reach ~39M vials (and that’s close to the company’s estimate too).
  • Starting from a $250 list price for DefenCath per vial, I assume a price reduction after the expiration of NTAP (New Technology Add-On Payment) and TDAPA (Transitional Drug Add-On Payment), combined with some political pressure and other discounts, to $80 per vial. That gives 39M vials x $80 = $3.12B.
  • As mentioned in my initial article, there are other catheter-lock solutions on the market, other potential new entrants, and even potential new treatments in the future. With their first-mover advantage, I assume 20%-25% market share, for a total of ~$750M peak sales.
  • I will go one step further with my rather conservative approach, because there is a possibility for their new US patent expiring in 2042 to be challenged. Therefore, I will stick with $700M for 2033 and 2034.

However, the vast majority of Melinta’s drugs are antibiotics and antifungals developed and commercialized specifically for use in hospital and acute care settings, a highly competitive market with moderate growth, due to complex procedures and limited payments for hospitals. CorMedix estimates that Melinta’s revenues will grow from $120M in 2024 to $125M-$135M in 2025. Therefore, I will model a gradually slower growth from ~7% to 1% in 2034.

Let’s move to the combined pipeline:

In my initial article, I modeled $160M peak sales for DefenCath TPN, in line with $150M to $200M seen by the company. Since it’s not approved yet, I would normally ponder it with a probability factor. However, there are other possible indications for DefenCath (see Figure #2); therefore, I stick with $160M.

Regarding the potential use of Rezzayo for the prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections in adult patients undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant, the company thinks that peak sales for this indication could exceed $200M. However, this time I will take a probability factor of 80% since it’s not approved yet. Then, with increased political pressure on drug prices, I will model only $120M peak sales, net of royalties. Besides, there are up to $25M milestone payments if the FDA approves the expanded indication of Rezzayo, so I assume that these will offset 2027 sales.

Financial Analysis

In a September investor presentation, CorMedix offered the following guidance for the combined revenue:

However, two days ago (October 20), the company has announced strong preliminary unaudited third-quarter financial results and a significant update to its full-year outlook, from $325M-$350M to at least $375M.

Based on the numbers from the above section along with this short-term adjustment, my peak sales estimates are:

Switching to profitability, I explained that I expect gross margin to go down a little bit after TDAPA expiration to a more normal level of ~78%. Melinta also came with a healthy 77% gross margin, and I will use 77% as a terminal gross margin.

Melinta did have huge SG&A expenses, with close to zero operating margin, but CorMedix is touting some normal post-merger synergies. I do, however, decrease my 2034 EBIT margin estimate to 42.2%, especially due to higher SG&A with this diversity of products.

Finally, let’s look a bit at CorMedix’s new convertible debt that they took to fund this acquisition. The interest rate is 4%, and the conversion price is ~$13.5, which is a little OTM (Out-of-The-Money), but not by much. Therefore, I will model half of it as debt (usually modelled for deep OTM conversion prices) and half of it as equity (considering a conversion).

Fair Value

I use 11.55% WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital), mostly coming from 12.5% cost of equity (about 85% in the capital structure). I use this high cost of equity because CorMedix still relies more than 50% on a single product. This diversification brought by Melinta is good, but I estimated about 73% of 2034 revenue from DefenCath.

I generally rate these high-reward, high-risk stocks as “Strong Buy” if:

  • There is more than 100% potential upside to my base-case Fair Value estimate. In this case there is ~147% potential upside from yesterday’s closing price ($11.2) to Fair Value ($27.8).
  • The stock is still undervalued in a slightly pessimistic scenario, and indeed, there is ~35% potential upside in this case.
  • The company does not rely on a single product. This is still on the verge, but I already count it in Cost of equity; therefore, I will not count it here again.

A Strategy Before Earnings

If we look at IV Rank, IV (Implied Volatility) seems to be moderate for CorMedix:

However, there is a very clear pattern, with IV climbing just before earnings, especially for short-term options, and the next earnings report is expected on November 13th. IV30 (Implied Volatility for options expiring in 30 days) is about 76% now, but it was, on average, more than 108% before earnings. Maybe it won’t be like that now because they already announced some pre-earnings results; therefore, I expect somewhere around 100%.

IV60 (that means options expiring in December) is around 72% now, but here is an interesting thing: on November 13th, these options expiring in December will have only ~36 days until expiration, much closer to IV30. Therefore, I expect their IV to go to ~95%, which means about a 33% increase.

And when I find a stock seriously undervalued and I expect IV to go up, I sometimes buy an ATM (At-The-Money) Call option, with the intention to sell it just before earnings, when IV will be higher. This is why I bought in the last two days some 12 Call Dec’25 options, for an average of $106. Let's see how they would perform just before earnings with that 33% expected increase in IV:

We can observe that break-even is slightly lower at $10.8-$10.9. Despite the normal time decay for any short-term long options, the higher IV should more than offset that.

An alternative is to buy a Call option expiring March ’26, about 5 months from now, for an average bid-ask premium of ~$213. In this case, time decay will be much slower, but also the jump in the IV just before earnings will be much less pronounced (I expect 5% to 10%). ROI should be lower for increasing stock prices, due to a higher cost, but also the loss should be lower for decreasing stock prices for the same reason:
